sections.header: Verses 1–12 — Mountain Goats, Wild Donkey, Wild Ox

heb.paragraph: "Who let the wild donkey (pereʾ) go free? Who untied its ropes?" (v.5). God liberated the wild donkey. It roams free, laughs at the town, and scorns the driver’s shout (v.7). God made creatures that serve no human purpose and are not under human control. The wild donkey is free because God made it free.

refs.note: "He makes springs pour water into the ravines... the wild donkeys quench their thirst" — God’s provision for wild creatures.
"Are not two sparrows sold for a penny? Yet not one of them will fall to the ground outside your Father’s care" — the same divine attention to animals.

notes.note: "Do you know when the mountain goats give birth? Do you watch when the doe bears her fawn?" Wildlife reproduces without human assistance. The natural world has God as its obstetrician.

"Who let the wild donkey go free? He laughs at the commotion in the town; he does not hear a driver’s shout." Freedom from human control is God’s gift to this creature. Not everything that exists is for us.
"Will the wild ox consent to serve you? Will it stay by your manger at night?" The wild ox is untameable. Its power serves God’s design, not human agriculture.
Calvin: the wild donkey’s freedom is a parable of grace. God gives some creatures freedom precisely because freedom is good, even when it serves no utilitarian purpose.
Calvin: the untameable ox teaches Job that power exists beyond human management. So does God’s sovereign action in Job’s suffering.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET: pereʾ is the Asian wild ass (onager), famous for its speed and untameability. It lives in salt flats and barren land where domesticated donkeys cannot survive.
The NET: the wild ox (rēʾem) is likely the now-extinct aurochs — ancestor of domestic cattle but vastly larger and completely untameable.

clines.source: David J.A. Clines, Job, WBC (3 vols., 1989–2011) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Clines: the wild donkey is God’s most subversive creation in this speech. It laughs at civilisation. It mocks the human project of domestication. God is proud of its wildness.
Clines: the wild ox won’t serve Job. Its strength is real but not available for human use. The implied lesson: God’s power, like the wild ox’s, serves divine purposes, not human ones.

habel.source: Norman C. Habel, The Book of Job, OTL (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Habel: God presents evidence from the animal kingdom. Each creature represents a domain God governs without human consultation. The judge demonstrates jurisdictions the plaintiff never knew existed.

hist.context: God’s second round of animal questions. Mountain goats give birth without human midwives (vv.1–4). The wild donkey roams free (vv.5–8). The wild ox cannot be domesticated (vv.9–12). Every animal challenges human centrality: these creatures exist outside the human economy. They serve God’s purposes, not ours. Job’s demand that the universe make sense to HIM is the wrong starting point.

sections.header: Verses 13–30 — The Ostrich, the Warhorse, the Eagle

heb.paragraph: "Do you give the horse (sūs) its strength? Do you clothe its neck with a flowing mane?" (v.19). The warhorse is God’s masterpiece of designed ferocity. It paws the ground, charges into battle, laughs at fear, does not shy from the sword. It was not made for Job’s use but for its own terrible glory.

refs.note: "The horse is made ready for the day of battle, but victory rests with the LORD" — the horse imagery connecting battle to divine sovereignty.
"Like an eagle that stirs up its nest and hovers over its young" — God compared to the eagle he describes here.

notes.note: "The wings of the ostrich flap joyfully, though they cannot compare with the wings of the stork. She lays her eggs on the ground... she is hardened toward her young. Yet when she spreads her feathers to run, she laughs at horse and rider." The ostrich: foolish by design, fast by design. Not everything in creation is optimised for wisdom.

"Do you give the horse its strength? It laughs at fear, afraid of nothing. It does not shy from the sword. It paws fiercely, rejoicing in its strength; it charges into the fray." The warhorse: designed for battle, exulting in violence. God made this. Not everything in creation is gentle.

"Does the hawk take flight by your wisdom? Does the eagle soar at your command? It dwells on a cliff... its young ones feast on blood." The eagle: predatory, high, feeding on death. God’s creation includes predation at the heights.
Calvin: the warhorse’s joy in battle is God-given. Not all divine gifts are gentle. Some are terrible. God’s creativity includes power, wildness, and even what we call savagery.

Calvin: the eagle feeding on blood is not a flaw in creation but a feature. God’s ecosystem includes death. This is not a fallen world here; this is how God designed it. The implication for Job: suffering may be part of the design, not a departure from it.

net.source: NET Bible Full Notes Edition — Biblical Studies Press

notes.note: The NET discusses the ostrich at length — the Hebrew is difficult and debated. The key point: God takes credit for a creature that lacks wisdom but has other gifts.
The NET: the warhorse passage is widely regarded as one of the finest examples of Hebrew poetry. The language mimics the horse’s energy — short, staccato phrases charging forward.

The NET: "its young ones feast on blood" is the speech’s final image before God demands Job’s response. The last picture is of death feeding life — the cycle of nature that transcends human moral categories.

clines.source: David J.A. Clines, Job, WBC (3 vols., 1989–2011) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Clines: the ostrich is God’s comedy. "God did not endow her with wisdom" (v.17). Yet she outruns horses. God deliberately created something foolish and fast. The design includes what we would call flaws.
Clines: the warhorse passage is the most exhilarating poetry in the book. God describes with obvious pleasure a creature that exists for violence. The divine aesthetic includes ferocity.

Clines: "where the slain are, there it is" — the eagle is drawn to death. God’s creation is not a nursery; it is a food chain. The whirlwind speeches present a wilder, more dangerous, more real God than the friends’ theology could accommodate.

habel.source: Norman C. Habel, The Book of Job, OTL (1985) — Scholarly Paraphrase

notes.note: Habel: God’s final animal witnesses include the foolish, the violent, and the predatory. The creation is not morally tidy. The retribution principle cannot account for an ostrich. The friends’ theology is too small for God’s world.

hist.context: The animal gallery concludes with three extraordinary creatures. The ostrich (vv.13–18): foolish by design, leaves eggs unattended, yet outruns the horse. The warhorse (vv.19–25): terrifying in battle, exulting in its own power. The hawk and eagle (vv.26–30): soaring beyond human reach, their young lapping up blood. God’s creation includes foolishness, violence, and predation — by design, not by accident.

ppl.name: Job
ppl.text: Job’s demand that the universe make sense to HIM is the wrong starting point…

ppl.text: God’s second round of animal questions…

Who has loosed the bonds of the swift donkey,NIVI gave it the wasteland as its home, the salt flats as its habitat.ESVto whom I have given the arid plain for his home and the salt land for his dwelling place?

rec.text: ""Do you know when the mountain goats give birth? Do you watch when the doe bears her fawn?" Wildlife reproduces without human assistance. The natural world has God as its obstetrician."

rows.label: Wild and Free — 39:1–12
rows.text: Mountain goats, wild donkeys, wild ox. Creatures that serve no human purpose. God is proud of their wildness.

rows.label: Foolish, Fierce, Predatory — 39:13–30
rows.text: Ostrich by design. Warhorse laughs at fear. Eagle’s young feast on blood.

hebtext.note: "Who let the wild donkey (pereʾ) go free? Who untied its ropes?" (v.5). God liberated the wild donkey. It roams free, laughs at the town, and scorns the driver’s shout (v.7). God made creatures that serve no human purpose and are not under human control. The wild donkey is free because God made it free.

"Do you give the horse (sūs) its strength? Do you clothe its neck with a flowing mane?" (v.19). The warhorse is God’s masterpiece of designed ferocity. It paws the ground, charges into battle, laughs at fear, does not shy from the sword. It was not made for Job’s use but for its own terrible glory.

thread.text: "He makes springs pour water into the ravines... the wild donkeys quench their thirst" — God’s provision for wild creatures.
"Are not two sparrows sold for a penny? Yet not one of them will fall to the ground outside your Father’s care" — the same divine attention to animals.
"The horse is made ready for the day of battle, but victory rests with the LORD" — the horse imagery connecting battle to divine sovereignty.
"Like an eagle that stirs up its nest and hovers over its young" — God compared to the eagle he describes here.

themes.note: Sovereignty maintains 10: God governs the wild kingdom. Holiness: the creation’s wildness reveals God’s untameable otherness.
